text.title: Automation Patterns: Batch Ops, Scheduled Scripts, Change History
text.summary: Patterns for batch keyword updates, scheduled daily performance checks with auto-pause, and change history monitoring via change_event.

section.title: Batch Operations

rule.statement: Process mutations in batches of up to 5,000 operations per request; never issue one API call per row.
rule.rationale: The Google Ads API allows up to 5,000 operations per mutate call. Single-operation loops exhaust quota and are orders of magnitude slower than batching.

section.reference: Guarded daily report job with retry and dry-run flag using search_stream

rule.statement: Before auto-pausing any keyword or ad group: enforce a minimum impression threshold (5,000+), minimum spend threshold, minimum age (7+ days), account allow-list, and emit a paused-list log per run.
rule.rationale: Auto-pause agents that trip on day-1 of a fresh campaign or on low-data keywords kill momentum without evidence. Each guardrail prevents a distinct failure mode.

antipattern.description: Running an auto-pause agent that fires within the first 7 days of campaign launch or without an impression floor.
antipattern.reason: New campaigns need time to accumulate data. Pausing too early based on low-sample statistics produces false negatives and disrupts Smart Bidding learning.

rule.statement: Build a change_event archive job from day one; run it daily because change_event only retains 30 days of history.
rule.rationale: When something unexpectedly breaks, the first diagnostic question is "who changed what and when." Missing even a single day of change_event creates permanent audit gaps.

rule.statement: Filter the change_event query to the specific resource type needed; broad queries on large accounts return thousands of rows and burn quota.
rule.rationale: change_event queries without a resource_type filter return all changes across all entity types. On large accounts this easily exceeds result limits and wastes daily quota.

rule.statement: One agent run equals one query batch equals one sink write; do not multiplex tasks in a single API session.
rule.rationale: Multiplexed sessions complicate retry logic, make partial-failure handling ambiguous, and produce inconsistent snapshots when one query succeeds and another fails.

rule.statement: Include account-level filters (customer.status = ENABLED) when iterating MCC trees; cancelled accounts return malformed shapes.
rule.rationale: MCC trees contain accounts in various states. Cancelled or suspended accounts return partial data that breaks downstream aggregations without obvious errors.
